narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and personal factors from past students. The model estimated a 61.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to succeed strongly. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning you placed in the bottom tenth of your peers that year—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average of around the 5th or 6th decile. This low performance stood out as the strongest signal to the model that bar exam success might be challenging. Next in importance was your LSAT score of 41, which actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward passing, since it's higher than the average of 37 seen in the dataset and suggests solid test-taking ability. Rounding out the top influences, your Year 1 decile of 2—again in the bottom tenth compared to the average 6th decile—nudged the prediction further toward failure, highlighting early academic struggles that echoed your later ones. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.4, below the typical 3.2, and full-time student status, which is common for 93% of applicants, played supporting roles but weren't the main drivers. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having a stronger LSAT but weaker early and late high school rankings, tipping the balance against you despite some positives. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted effort. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger high school performance or additional coursework, retake the LSAT if possible to solidify that strength, and consider programs that support undergrad GPA improvement like post-baccalaureate classes. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds significantly, and schools value persistence in applicants like you.
